lines.translation: After arriving in Beijing, Lisa didn't live in the international student dorm. To better understand the lives of Chinese people, she moved into a Chinese home. The landlord couple is retired, and their daughter, Li Wen, works at a museum and lives with her parents. Li Wen really wants to study in the US, so she asked Lisa to be her English tutor. This way, Lisa doesn't have to pay rent. Because Lisa speaks Chinese with Li Wen's parents every day, her Chinese has improved very quickly.

Every morning, Lisa goes out for a walk. In her residential community, on the streets, and in the parks, she can see many people, especially seniors, exercising. Some of them do square dancing, and some practice tai chi. These people who exercise in the morning have become a special 'scenery' of Beijing.
This morning, just as Lisa was about to go out, Li Wen came out of her room.
Lisa, it's Saturday today. How come you're up so early?
I was planning to learn tai chi with uncle and auntie, but I didn't expect them to have left so early.
I thought you only liked to do yoga. How come you're also interested in tai chi?
Tai chi is like yoga; not only is it good for your health, but the movements are also very beautiful.
Lisa, look under the big tree. My dad and mom are practicing tai chi with those retired seniors! Go find them.
Okay, I'll go in a bit. They practice so well. Every morning when I go for a walk, I see people exercising everywhere. Chinese people really value physical exercise!
Right, everyone is paying more and more attention to their health now. Hey, Lisa, how do Americans usually exercise?
Usually it's running, swimming, playing ball, and so on. Some people also go to the gym. I usually do yoga, and sometimes I run too.
No wonder you are so healthy.
I think if you want to be healthy, besides exercising more, you should also pay attention to your diet.
You're not fat at all. Do you still need to pay attention to your diet?
Paying attention to diet isn't the same as losing weight. I think as long as you're healthy, being fat or thin isn't important.
I'm busy with work and don't have time to eat properly, so I often eat whatever. How do you pay attention to your diet?

Drink more water, eat more vegetables and fruits. Also, even if you're very busy, you must eat breakfast, and it should be nutritious. You should eat a full lunch because you still have to study and work in the afternoon. For dinner, you should eat a little less, otherwise you'll get fatter and fatter, because it's too close to bedtime.
We Chinese have a saying: 'Eat a good breakfast, a full lunch, and a small dinner,' which is pretty much the same as what you said.
Really? Everyone says that, maybe there's a scientific reason for it.
I think if you want to be healthy, you must also pay attention to other aspects.
You mean to have good lifestyle habits, right?

Right. Don't smoke, don't drink, sleep early and wake up early, and it's best not to stay up late. I have no problem with the others, but I just can't manage not staying up late. I often have to burn the midnight oil to prepare for the graduate entrance exams.
Then you must find time to catch up on sleep as much as possible.
You're right, I really need to pay attention to it, otherwise my two eyes will soon turn into panda eyes.

example_sentence.cn: 丽莎到北京后没有住留学生公寓，为了更多地了解中国人的生活，她住进了一个中国家庭。房东夫妻二人已退休，女儿李文在博物馆工作，与父母住在一起。李文很想去美国留学，所以请丽莎做她的英文家教，这样丽莎就不必付房租了。因为丽莎每天都跟李文的父母说中文，所以她的中文进步得很快。
example_sentence.py: Lìshā dào Běijīng hòu méiyǒu zhù liúxuéshēng gōngyù wèile gèng duō de liǎojiě Zhōngguórén de shēnghuó tā zhùjìn le yí ge Zhōngguó jiātíng fángdōng fūqī èr rén yǐ tuìxiū nǚ'ér Lǐ Wén zài bówùguǎn gōngzuò yǔ fùmǔ zhù zài yìqǐ Lǐ Wén hěn xiǎng qù Měiguó liúxué suǒyǐ qǐng Lìshā zuò tā de Yīngwén jiājiào zhèyàng Lìshā jiù búbì fù fángzū le yīnwèi Lìshā měitiān dōu gēn Lǐ Wén de fùmǔ shuō Zhōngwén suǒyǐ tā de Zhōngwén jìnbù de hěn kuài
example_sentence.translation: After arriving in Beijing, Lisa didn't live in the international student dorm. To better understand the lives of Chinese people, she moved into a Chinese home. The landlord couple is retired, and their daughter, Li Wen, works at a museum and lives with her parents. Li Wen really wants to study in the US, so she asked Lisa to be her English tutor. This way, Lisa doesn't have to pay rent. Because Lisa speaks Chinese with Li Wen's parents every day, her Chinese has improved very quickly.

The landlord couple is retired, and their daughter, Li Wen, works at a museum and lives with their parents. Li Wen really wants to study in the U.S., so she asked Lisha to be her English tutor. This way, Lisha doesn't have to pay rent. Because Lisha speaks Chinese with Li Wen's parents every day, her Chinese has improved very quickly.

language_notes.label: a
language_notes.explanation_en: 在早晨进行练习或锻炼 can be shortened to 晨练.

language_notes.label: b
language_notes.explanation_en: 瑜伽 can also be written as 瑜珈. The pronunciation is the same.

language_notes.label: c
language_notes.focus_text: 早餐, 午餐, 晚餐
language_notes.explanation_en: 早餐, 午餐, and 晚餐 are interchangeable with 早饭, 午饭, and 晚饭, respectively. 餐 sounds more formal than 饭.

language_notes.label: d
language_notes.explanation_en: The negative form of 必须 is 不必 or 不用.

language_notes.label: e
language_notes.explanation_en: The simplified character 烟 corresponds to two traditional variants, 烟 and 烟. Although 烟 and 烟 are sometimes used interchangeably, 烟 is recommended when referring to the tobacco plant and tobacco-related products and activities, as in 烟草 (yāncǎo) (tobacco), 香烟 (xiāngyān) (cigarette), 吸烟 (to smoke), and 禁烟 (jìn yān) (to ban smoking). By contrast, 烟 is associated primarily with smoke and smoke-like vapor or mist, as in 炊烟 (chuīyān) (cooking smoke) and 烟雾 (yānwù) (clouds and mist). Note that 烟 and 烟 have different radicals: 火 (huǒ) (fire) and 艹 (cǎo) (grass), respectively. 烟草, 香烟, 吸烟, and 禁烟 are acceptable; 炊烟 and 烟雾 are not.

language_notes.label: f
language_notes.explanation_en: 开夜车 (lit. to drive the night car) means "to stay up late (to work or study)."

language_notes.label: g
language_notes.explanation_en: The term 熊猫 (lit. bear cat) is used in Mainland China. However, in Taiwan, the term 猫熊 (lit. cat bear) is preferred.

grammar_sections.title: Monosyllabic forms of disyllabic words
grammar_sections.explanation_en: Two-syllable modal verbs, adverbs, conjunctions, and so on often appear in monosyllabic form in formal Chinese. Some examples are:

grammar_sections.title: The conjunction and preposition 与
grammar_sections.explanation_en: 与 is a conjunction as well as a preposition. It appears in formal Chinese. As a conjunction, it is synonymous with 和 and 跟. Like 和 and 跟, 与 can serve as a preposition.

examples.translation: Li Wen lives with her parents.

examples.hanzi: 丽莎与天明一起去云南旅行。
examples.translation: Lisha travels to Yunnan with Tianming.

examples.translation: My shopping criteria are different from yours.

grammar_sections.title: 只要…就…
grammar_sections.explanation_en: 在“只要…就…”这个句型中，“只要”引出必要条件，“就”后面的词语则表示在这一条件下产生的结果。

patterns.pattern: 只要 + 必要条件, 就 + 结果

examples.hanzi: 小林租房子,只要环境好就行,其他的他都不在乎。
examples.pinyin: Xiǎo Lín zū fángzi, zhǐyào huánjìng hǎo jiù xíng, qítā de tā dōu bú zàihu.
examples.translation: In looking for an apartment, Little Lin is okay with renting anything as long as the environment is good. He doesn't care about anything else.

examples.hanzi: 只要你好好儿用功学习,你的中文就一定会进步。
examples.pinyin: Zhǐyào nǐ hǎohāor yònggōng xuéxí, nǐ de Zhōngwén jiù yídìng huì jìnbù.
examples.translation: As long as you study diligently, your Chinese will definitely improve.

examples.hanzi: 很多父母认为,只要孩子平安、健康,就是自己最大的幸福。
examples.pinyin: Hěn duō fùmǔ rènwéi, zhǐyào háizi píng'ān, jiànkāng, jiù shì zìjǐ zuì dà de xìngfú.
examples.translation: Many parents consider their greatest happiness to be the safety and health of their children.

grammar_sections.title: 即使…也…
grammar_sections.explanation_en: In the structure “即使…也…,” 即使… usually presents an extreme scenario, and 也… indicates the result or conclusion that will be reached even if the previously mentioned hypothetical occurs.

patterns.pattern: 即使 + Hypothetical Scenario, (Subject) + 也 + Result/Conclusion

examples.hanzi: 即使熬夜不睡觉,我也要把这篇文章写完。
examples.pinyin: Jíshǐ áoyè bù shuìjiào, wǒ yě yào bǎ zhè piān wénzhāng xiě wán.
examples.translation: Even if I [have to] stay up all night, I will finish writing this article.

examples.hanzi: 我啊,即使不吃东西只喝水,也会胖。
examples.pinyin: Wǒ a, jíshǐ bù chī dōngxi zhǐ hē shuǐ, yě huì pàng.
examples.translation: For me, even if I didn't eat anything and just drank water, I'd still put on weight.

examples.hanzi: 他的事业非常成功,但是太忙。即使是感恩节,也在办公室工作。
examples.pinyin: Tā de shìyè fēicháng chénggōng, dànshì tài máng. Jíshǐ shì Gǎn'ēnjié, yě zài bàngōngshì gōngzuò.
examples.translation: He's been very successful in his career, but he is too busy. He has to work in the office even on Thanksgiving.

global_config.visual_style: Cinematic, realistic, bright and warm lighting, slice-of-life feel.
global_config.setting_summary_cn: 一个典型的北京家庭住宅、附近的公园和小区，展现了美国留学生丽莎在中国家庭的生活以及她对中国晨练文化的观察。
global_config.setting_summary_en: A typical Beijing family home, a nearby park, and a residential community, showcasing the life of an American student, Lisa, in a Chinese family and her observations of Chinese morning exercise culture.

character_definitions.name: 丽莎
character_definitions.appearance_description_en: American woman in her early 20s, blonde hair, blue eyes, friendly and curious expression. Wears casual, comfortable clothing. In morning scenes, she wears sporty attire like a tracksuit or yoga pants.

character_definitions.name: 李文
character_definitions.appearance_description_en: Chinese woman in her early 20s, black hair, intelligent and gentle look. Wears stylish but modest clothes. In the morning scene, she is in her pajamas or comfortable loungewear.

character_definitions.name: 房东夫妻
character_definitions.appearance_description_en: A kind-looking Chinese couple in their 60s, retired. The husband and wife have graying hair and wear simple, comfortable clothes. They appear warm and friendly.

hanzi: 丽莎到北京后没有住留学生公寓，为了更多地了解中国人的生活，她住进了一个中国家庭。房东夫妻二人已退休，女儿李文在博物馆工作，与父母住在一起。李文很想去美国留学，所以请丽莎做她的英文家教，这样丽莎就不必付房租了。因为丽莎每天都跟李文的父母说中文，所以她的中文进步得很快。
pinyin: Lìshā dào Běijīng hòu méiyǒu zhù liúxuéshēng gōngyù wèile gèng duō de liǎojiě Zhōngguórén de shēnghuó tā zhùjìn le yí ge Zhōngguó jiātíng fángdōng fūqī èr rén yǐ tuìxiū nǚ'ér Lǐ Wén zài bówùguǎn gōngzuò yǔ fùmǔ zhù zài yìqǐ Lǐ Wén hěn xiǎng qù Měiguó liúxué suǒyǐ qǐng Lìshā zuò tā de Yīngwén jiājiào zhèyàng Lìshā jiù búbì fù fángzū le yīnwèi Lìshā měitiān dōu gēn Lǐ Wén de fùmǔ shuō Zhōngwén suǒyǐ tā de Zhōngwén jìnbù de hěn kuài
translation: After arriving in Beijing, Lisa didn't live in the international student dorm. To better understand the lives of Chinese people, she moved into a Chinese home. The landlord couple is retired, and their daughter, Li Wen, works at a museum and lives with her parents. Li Wen really wants to study in the US, so she asked Lisa to be her English tutor. This way, Lisa doesn't have to pay rent. Because Lisa speaks Chinese with Li Wen's parents every day, her Chinese has improved very quickly.

scenes.scene_goal: 介绍主角丽莎的背景和她独特的在华居住安排。
scenes.scene_description_cn: 通过一系列快速蒙太奇镜头，展现丽莎住进中国家庭、教李文英文、以及和房东夫妻用中文愉快交流的场景，交代故事背景。
scenes.setting_en: A cozy, traditional Chinese apartment in Beijing.
scenes.video_prompt_en: Montage sequence: 1. A young American woman, Lisa, with a suitcase, is warmly welcomed into a cozy Chinese apartment by a friendly elderly couple. 2. Lisa and a young Chinese woman, Li Wen, are sitting at a desk, Lisa is pointing at an English book, tutoring her. 3. Lisa is sitting at a dinner table with the elderly couple, chatting and laughing in Mandarin. The atmosphere is warm and familial. Cinematic, slice-of-life.

voice_over.speaker: 旁白
voice_over.hanzi: 丽莎到北京后没有住留学生公寓，为了更多地了解中国人的生活，她住进了一个中国家庭。房东夫妻二人已退休，女儿李文在博物馆工作，与父母住在一起。李文很想去美国留学，所以请丽莎做她的英文家教，这样丽莎就不必付房租了。因为丽莎每天都跟李文的父母说中文，所以她的中文进步得很快。
voice_over.pinyin: Lìshā dào Běijīng hòu méiyǒu zhù liúxuéshēng gōngyù wèile gèng duō de liǎojiě Zhōngguórén de shēnghuó tā zhùjìn le yí ge Zhōngguó jiātíng fángdōng fūqī èr rén yǐ tuìxiū nǚ'ér Lǐ Wén zài bówùguǎn gōngzuò yǔ fùmǔ zhù zài yìqǐ Lǐ Wén hěn xiǎng qù Měiguó liúxué suǒyǐ qǐng Lìshā zuò tā de Yīngwén jiājiào zhèyàng Lìshā jiù búbì fù fángzū le yīnwèi Lìshā měitiān dōu gēn Lǐ Wén de fùmǔ shuō Zhōngwén suǒyǐ tā de Zhōngwén jìnbù de hěn kuài
voice_over.translation: After arriving in Beijing, Lisa didn't live in the international student dorm. To better understand the lives of Chinese people, she moved into a Chinese home. The landlord couple is retired, and their daughter, Li Wen, works at a museum and lives with her parents. Li Wen really wants to study in the US, so she asked Lisa to be her English tutor. This way, Lisa doesn't have to pay rent. Because Lisa speaks Chinese with Li Wen's parents every day, her Chinese has improved very quickly.

hanzi: 丽莎每天早上都出去散步，在她住的这个小区，在街边，在公园，都能看见很多人，特别是老人，在锻炼身体。他们有的跳广场舞，有的打太极拳。这些早晨锻炼的人们，成为北京特别的“风景”。
pinyin: Lìshā měitiān zǎoshang dōu chūqù sànbù zài tā zhù de zhège xiǎoqū zài jiēbiān zài gōngyuán dōu néng kànjiàn hěnduō rén tèbié shì lǎorén zài duànliàn shēntǐ tāmen yǒude tiào guǎngchǎngwǔ yǒude dǎ tàijíquán zhèxiē zǎochen duànliàn de rénmen chéngwéi Běijīng tèbié de fēngjǐng
translation: Every morning, Lisa goes out for a walk. In her residential community, on the streets, and in the parks, she can see many people, especially seniors, exercising. Some of them do square dancing, and some practice tai chi. These people who exercise in the morning have become a special 'scenery' of Beijing.

scenes.scene_goal: 展现丽莎对北京晨练文化的观察和好奇。
scenes.scene_description_cn: 清晨，丽莎穿着运动服在公园里散步，她好奇地看着周围锻炼的人群，有跳广场舞的大妈和打太极拳的老人。镜头从丽莎的视角展现这片独特的“风景”。
scenes.setting_en: A bustling Beijing park in the early morning.
scenes.camera_movement: Tracking shot following Lisa, then pans across the park.
scenes.video_prompt_en: Early morning, soft golden light. Lisa, in a tracksuit, is jogging slowly through a beautiful Beijing park. She looks around with curiosity and a smile. The camera pans to show groups of elderly people exercising: one group is energetically doing square dancing with colorful fans, another group is gracefully practicing Tai Chi under a large tree. The scene is vibrant and full of life.

voice_over.speaker: 旁白
voice_over.hanzi: 丽莎每天早上都出去散步，在她住的这个小区，在街边，在公园，都能看见很多人，特别是老人，在锻炼身体。他们有的跳广场舞，有的打太极拳。这些早晨锻炼的人们，成为北京特别的“风景”。
voice_over.pinyin: Lìshā měitiān zǎoshang dōu chūqù sànbù zài tā zhù de zhège xiǎoqū zài jiēbiān zài gōngyuán dōu néng kànjiàn hěnduō rén tèbié shì lǎorén zài duànliàn shēntǐ tāmen yǒude tiào guǎngchǎngwǔ yǒude dǎ tàijíquán zhèxiē zǎochen duànliàn de rénmen chéngwéi Běijīng tèbié de fēngjǐng
voice_over.translation: Every morning, Lisa goes out for a walk. In her residential community, on the streets, and in the parks, she can see many people, especially seniors, exercising. Some of them do square dancing, and some practice tai chi. These people who exercise in the morning have become a special 'scenery' of Beijing.

hanzi: 这一天早上丽莎正要出门，李文从房间走出来。
丽莎，今天是星期六，怎么这么早就起床了？
pinyin: zhè yìtiān zǎoshang Lìshā zhèngyào chūmén Lǐ Wén cóng fángjiān zǒu chūlái
Lìshā jīntiān shì xīngqīliù zěnme zhème zǎo jiù qǐchuáng le
translation: This morning, just as Lisa was about to go out, Li Wen came out of her room.
Lisa, it's Saturday today. How come you're up so early?

scenes.scene_goal: 开启丽莎和李文关于早起和锻炼的对话。
scenes.scene_description_cn: 周六清晨，在房东家的客厅里，丽莎穿着运动服正准备出门。李文穿着睡衣、睡眼惺忪地从自己房间走出来，看到丽莎后惊讶地问她为什么起这么早。
scenes.setting_en: The living room of the Chinese family's apartment, early Saturday morning.
scenes.camera_movement: Static shot, then a slight pan to follow Li Wen.
scenes.video_prompt_en: Interior, living room, early Saturday morning. Lisa, dressed in sportswear, is tying her shoelaces near the front door. Li Wen, in cute pajamas and looking sleepy, walks out of her bedroom, yawning. She sees Lisa and looks surprised.

voice_over.speaker: 李文
voice_over.hanzi: 丽莎，今天是星期六，怎么这么早就起床了？
voice_over.pinyin: Lìshā jīntiān shì xīngqīliù zěnme zhème zǎo jiù qǐchuáng le
voice_over.translation: Lisa, it's Saturday today. How come you're up so early?

hanzi: 我本来想跟叔叔、阿姨去学太极拳，没想到他们那么早就出去了。
我以为你只喜欢做瑜伽，怎么对太极拳也有兴趣？
太极拳和瑜伽一样，不但对身体有好处，而且动作很美。
pinyin: wǒ běnlái xiǎng gēn shūshu āyí qù xué tàijíquán méi xiǎngdào tāmen nàme zǎo jiù chūqù le
wǒ yǐwéi nǐ zhǐ xǐhuān zuò yújiā zěnme duì tàijíquán yě yǒu xìngqù
tàijíquán hé yújiā yíyàng búdàn duì shēntǐ yǒu hǎochu érqiě dòngzuò hěn měi
translation: I was planning to learn tai chi with uncle and auntie, but I didn't expect them to have left so early.
I thought you only liked to do yoga. How come you're also interested in tai chi?
Tai chi is like yoga; not only is it good for your health, but the movements are also very beautiful.

scenes.scene_goal: 丽莎表达对太极拳的兴趣，并将其与瑜伽进行比较。
scenes.scene_description_cn: 丽莎微笑着回答李文，解释自己想学太极拳。李文表示惊讶。丽莎一边说，一边模仿了一个优美的瑜伽动作，然后又模仿了一个太极拳的起手式，来表现两者的相似之处。
scenes.setting_en: The living room of the Chinese family's apartment, early Saturday morning.
scenes.camera_movement: Cut between close-ups of Lisa and Li Wen to capture their expressions.
scenes.video_prompt_en: Continuing the scene. Lisa turns to Li Wen with a slightly disappointed but cheerful look. As Li Wen asks her question with a curious tilt of her head, Lisa smiles. When Lisa says 'Tai chi and yoga are alike', she gracefully performs a simple, beautiful yoga pose, then transitions into a slow, flowing Tai Chi opening move. Li Wen watches with growing interest.

voice_over.speaker: 丽莎
voice_over.hanzi: 太极拳和瑜伽一样，不但对身体有好处，而且动作很美。
voice_over.pinyin: tàijíquán hé yújiā yíyàng búdàn duì shēntǐ yǒu hǎochu érqiě dòngzuò hěn měi
voice_over.translation: Tai chi is like yoga; not only is it good for your health, but the movements are also very beautiful.

hanzi: 真的？大家都这么说，可能是有科学道理的。
我觉得想要身体好，在其他方面也必须注意。
pinyin: zhēn de? dà jiā dōu zhè me shuō, kě néng shì yǒu kē xué dào lǐ de.
wǒ jué de xiǎng yào shēn tǐ hǎo, zài qí tā fāng miàn yě bì xū zhù yì.
translation: Really? Everyone says that, maybe there's a scientific reason for it.
I think if you want to be healthy, you must also pay attention to other aspects.

scenes.scene_goal: 将话题从饮食俗语自然过渡到其他健康方面。
scenes.scene_description_cn: 丽莎听到中国俗语后，惊喜地表示认同。镜头转向李文，他点点头，表情变得若有所思，说出自己的想法，认为健康不止关乎饮食。
scenes.setting_en: Bright, modern cafe interior. They are sitting at a small wooden table with coffee cups.
scenes.camera_movement: Start on Lisa, then pan/cut to Li Wen as he speaks.
scenes.video_prompt_en: A young Asian woman (Lisa) in a cafe looks surprised and pleased. The camera then focuses on a young Asian man (Li Wen) across from her. He nods thoughtfully, his expression turning serious as he speaks, looking down at the table for a moment.

voice_over.speaker: 李文
voice_over.hanzi: 我觉得想要身体好，在其他方面也必须注意。
voice_over.pinyin: wǒ jué de xiǎng yào shēn tǐ hǎo, zài qí tā fāng miàn yě bì xū zhù yì.
voice_over.translation: I think if you want to be healthy, you must also pay attention to other aspects.

hanzi: 你的意思是要有良好的生活习惯，对不对？
pinyin: nǐ de yì si shì yào yǒu liáng hǎo de shēng huó xí guàn, duì bu duì?
translation: You mean to have good lifestyle habits, right?

scenes.scene_goal: 丽莎准确总结出李文的意思，表现出两人的默契。
scenes.scene_description_cn: 镜头切回丽莎，她身体微微前倾，带着一种“我懂你”的表情，向李文确认他的意思是不是指“良好的生活习惯”。
scenes.setting_en: Bright, modern cafe interior. At their table.
scenes.video_prompt_en: Medium close-up on the young Asian woman (Lisa). She leans forward slightly, with an intelligent and understanding expression, asking a clarifying question with a knowing look.

voice_over.speaker: 丽莎
voice_over.hanzi: 你的意思是要有良好的生活习惯，对不对？
voice_over.pinyin: nǐ de yì si shì yào yǒu liáng hǎo de shēng huó xí guàn, duì bu duì?
voice_over.translation: You mean to have good lifestyle habits, right?

hanzi: 对。不吸烟，不喝酒，早睡早起，最好不要熬夜。别的都没问题，就是不熬夜我做不到。我晚上常常得开夜车准备考研究生。
pinyin: duì. bù xīyān, bù hējiǔ, zǎo shuì zǎo qǐ, zuìhǎo búyào áoyè. biéde dōu méi wèntí, jiùshì bù áoyè wǒ zuòbudào. wǒ wǎnshang chángcháng děi kāi yèchē zhǔnbèi kǎo yánjiūshēng.
translation: Right. Don't smoke, don't drink, sleep early and wake up early, and it's best not to stay up late. I have no problem with the others, but I just can't manage not staying up late. I often have to burn the midnight oil to prepare for the graduate entrance exams.

scenes.scene_goal: 李文详细说明自己的困扰——因备考而熬夜，并通过视觉化蒙太奇来表现。
scenes.scene_description_cn: 李文点头同意。画面中出现快速的想象蒙太奇：一个“禁止吸烟”的标志、一只手推开酒杯。然后画面切到李文深夜在书桌前学习，打着哈欠，显得很疲惫。最后镜头切回咖啡馆，他无奈地摊开手，表情苦恼。
scenes.setting_en: Starts in the cafe, then a quick montage of symbolic shots, then a dark study room, then back to the cafe.
scenes.camera_movement: Quick cuts for the montage sequence.
scenes.video_prompt_en: Medium shot of Li Wen in the cafe, he nods. Quick montage: a 'no smoking' sign, a hand pushing away a glass of wine. Cut to a shot of Li Wen looking exhausted while studying late at night at a desk piled with books, yawning. Cut back to him in the cafe, shrugging with a helpless, frustrated expression.

voice_over.speaker: 李文
voice_over.hanzi: 对。不吸烟，不喝酒，早睡早起，最好不要熬夜。别的都没问题，就是不熬夜我做不到。我晚上常常得开夜车准备考研究生。
voice_over.pinyin: duì. bù xīyān, bù hējiǔ, zǎo shuì zǎo qǐ, zuìhǎo búyào áoyè. biéde dōu méi wèntí, jiùshì bù áoyè wǒ zuòbudào. wǒ wǎnshang chángcháng děi kāi yèchē zhǔnbèi kǎo yánjiūshēng.
voice_over.translation: Right. Don't smoke, don't drink, sleep early and wake up early, and it's best not to stay up late. I have no problem with the others, but I just can't manage not staying up late. I often have to burn the midnight oil to prepare for the graduate entrance exams.

hanzi: 那你必须尽可能找时间补充睡眠。
你说得对，我是得注意了，要不然我的两只眼睛都快变成熊猫眼了。
pinyin: nà nǐ bìxū jǐn kěnéng zhǎo shíjiān bǔchōng shuìmián.
nǐ shuō de duì, wǒ shì děi zhùyì le, yàoburán wǒ de liǎng zhī yǎnjing dōu kuài biànchéng xióngmāoyǎn le.
translation: Then you must find time to catch up on sleep as much as possible.
You're right, I really need to pay attention to it, otherwise my two eyes will soon turn into panda eyes.

scenes.scene_goal: 在丽莎的建议下，李文下定决心要注意睡眠，并用“熊猫眼”的比喻风趣地结束对话。
scenes.scene_description_cn: 丽莎关切地看着李文，给出补充睡眠的建议。李文听后，先是点头表示赞同，然后用手指了指自己的眼睛下方，做出一个夸张的疲惫表情，自嘲快有熊猫眼了。
scenes.setting_en: Bright, modern cafe interior. At their table.
scenes.camera_movement: Shot of Lisa, then cut to Li Wen's reaction.
scenes.video_prompt_en: A shot of Lisa looking concerned and giving advice. Cut to Li Wen, who nods in agreement, then points to under his eyes and makes a funny, exaggerated tired face, as if he has dark circles. He smiles wryly, looking self-deprecating.

voice_over.speaker: 李文
voice_over.hanzi: 你说得对，我是得注意了，要不然我的两只眼睛都快变成熊猫眼了。
voice_over.pinyin: nǐ shuō de duì, wǒ shì děi zhùyì le, yàoburán wǒ de liǎng zhī yǎnjing dōu kuài biànchéng xióngmāoyǎn le.
voice_over.translation: You're right, I really need to pay attention to it, otherwise my two eyes will soon turn into panda eyes.

global_config.teaching_style: A friendly and structured approach, breaking down sentences and vocabulary for clear understanding, with cultural context.
global_config.visual_style: Clean, card-based animations with text highlights and simple iconography.

segments.teaching_goal: Understand why Lisa chose to live with a Chinese family and learn the vocabulary for her decision.

narration.subtitle_en: Let's start with the background. This sentence explains Lisa's living situation in Beijing. It says: After arriving in Beijing, Lisa didn't live in the international student dorm; to better understand the lives of Chinese people, she moved into a Chinese home. The key phrase [zh:为了...了解] means 'in order to understand'. She chose a homestay over a dorm for a deeper cultural immersion. This is a great way to practice daily conversation and experience local life firsthand.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 丽莎到北京后没有住留学生公寓，为了更多地了解中国人的生活，她住进了一个中国家庭。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Lìshā dào Běijīng hòu méiyǒu zhù liúxuéshēng gōngyù, wèile gèng duō de liǎojiě Zhōngguórén de shēnghuó, tā zhùjìn le yí ge Zhōngguó jiātíng.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: After arriving in Beijing, Lisa didn't live in the international student dorm. To better understand the lives of Chinese people, she moved into a Chinese home.

segments.visual_notes: Animate an icon of a university dorm changing into a family home icon.

segments.teaching_goal: Learn key vocabulary to describe a family and their status.

narration.subtitle_en: Let's look at some words used to describe Lisa's host family. First, [zh:夫妻] means 'husband and wife' or 'a couple'. Next, [zh:退休] means 'to retire'. So, the landlord couple is retired. Their daughter works at a [zh:博物馆], which means 'museum'. Finally, the word [zh:与] is a formal way to say 'and' or 'with', similar to [zh:和] or [zh:跟]. It's often used in written or more formal spoken Chinese.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 夫妻 / 退休 / 博物馆 / 与
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: fūqī / tuìxiū / bówùguǎn / yǔ
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: husband and wife / to retire / museum / and; with

segments.visual_notes: Show each word with an accompanying icon: a couple, a person relaxing, a museum building, and a plus sign.

segments.teaching_goal: Understand the description of the host family members.

narration.subtitle_en: Now let's learn about the family. The text says: The landlord couple is retired, and their daughter, Li Wen, works at a museum and lives with her parents. We see the words we just learned: [zh:夫妻] for the couple, and [zh:退休] for retired. Notice the use of [zh:与], the formal word for 'with', in the phrase [zh:与父母住在一起], 'lives together with her parents'. The character [zh:已] is a formal, single-character version of [zh:已经], meaning 'already'.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 房东夫妻二人已退休，女儿李文在博物馆工作，与父母住在一起。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Fángdōng fūqī èr rén yǐ tuìxiū, nǚ'ér Lǐ Wén zài bówùguǎn gōngzuò, yǔ fùmǔ zhù zài yīqǐ.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: The landlord couple is retired, and their daughter, Li Wen, works at a museum and lives with her parents.
on_screen_text.notes: 已 (yǐ) is a formal version of 已经 (yǐjīng), meaning 'already'.

segments.visual_notes: Show a simple family tree diagram with the parents labeled '退休' and the daughter labeled '博物馆工作'.

segments.teaching_goal: Understand the arrangement between Lisa and Li Wen.

narration.subtitle_en: This part explains the special arrangement Lisa has. It says: Li Wen really wants to study in the US, so she asked Lisa to be her English tutor. This way, Lisa doesn't have to pay rent. The word [zh:所以] indicates a result, 'so'. The key word here is [zh:不必], which means 'need not' or 'don't have to'. It's the negative form of [zh:必须], 'must'. So, Lisa tutors English in exchange for free rent.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 李文很想去美国留学，所以请丽莎做她的英文家教，这样丽莎就不必付房租了。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Lǐ Wén hěn xiǎng qù Měiguó liúxué, suǒyǐ qǐng Lìshā zuò tā de Yīngwén jiājiào, zhèyàng Lìshā jiù búbì fù fángzū le.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Li Wen really wants to study in the US, so she asked Lisa to be her English tutor. This way, Lisa doesn't have to pay rent.
on_screen_text.notes: 不必 (búbì) means 'need not'. It is the negative form of 必须 (bìxū), 'must'.

segments.visual_notes: Animate an exchange icon: an 'English' book from Lisa to Li Wen, and a 'rent money' icon with a cross over it from Li Wen to Lisa.

segments.teaching_goal: Understand the cause-and-effect relationship described in the sentence.

narration.subtitle_en: Finally, the paragraph concludes with the positive outcome of this arrangement. The structure [zh:因为...所以...] means 'because... therefore...'. It says: Because Lisa speaks Chinese with Li Wen's parents every day, her Chinese has improved very quickly. [zh:进步] means 'to improve' or 'make progress', and [zh:得很快] describes the manner of the improvement—'very quickly'. This highlights the benefit of her immersive living situation.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 因为丽莎每天都跟李文的父母说中文，所以她的中文进步得很快。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Yīnwèi Lìshā měitiān dōu gēn Lǐ Wén de fùmǔ shuō Zhōngwén, suǒyǐ tā de Zhōngwén jìnbù de hěn kuài.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Because Lisa speaks Chinese with Li Wen's parents every day, her Chinese has improved very quickly.
on_screen_text.notes: 因为 (yīnwèi)... 所以 (suǒyǐ)... is a common structure for 'because... so...'.

segments.visual_notes: Show a graph with a line going up, labeled '中文水平' (Chinese level).

segments.teaching_goal: Learn how to describe a daily routine.

narration.subtitle_en: Now the scene shifts to Lisa's daily routine. This sentence says: Every morning, Lisa goes out for a walk. The key phrase is [zh:每天早上], 'every morning'. [zh:都] is an adverb that means 'all' or 'every', emphasizing that this happens every single morning. The action is [zh:出去散步], which means 'to go out for a walk'.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 丽莎每天早上都出去散步，
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Lìshā měitiān zǎoshang dōu chūqù sàn bù,
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Every morning, Lisa goes out for a walk,

segments.visual_notes: Animate a character walking out of a house as the sun rises.

segments.teaching_goal: Learn the verbs for taking a walk and exercising.

narration.subtitle_en: Let's focus on two important verbs related to health. First, [zh:散步] is a verb-object phrase meaning 'to take a walk'. [zh:散] means to 'disperse' or 'stroll', and [zh:步] means 'step'. Together, it's like taking leisurely steps. The second word is [zh:锻炼], which means 'to exercise' or 'to work out'. This is a more general term for physical training to keep your body, or [zh:身体], healthy.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 散步 / 锻炼
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: sàn bù / duànliàn
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: to take a walk / to exercise

highlight_words.character_insight_en: 散 (stroll) + 步 (step) = to take a walk.
A general term for physical training.

segments.visual_notes: Show an icon of a person walking for '散步' and an icon of a person lifting weights for '锻炼'.

segments.teaching_goal: Understand the description of the morning exercise scene in Beijing.

narration.subtitle_en: This sentence describes what Lisa sees on her walks. It lists several locations: [zh:小区] (residential community), [zh:街边] (on the street), and [zh:公园] (in the park). In all these places, she sees many people, [zh:特别是] 'especially', seniors ([zh:老人]), who are exercising, or [zh:锻炼身体]. This paints a vivid picture of the active morning culture in China.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 在她住的这个小区，在街边，在公园，都能看见很多人，特别是老人，在锻炼身体。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: zài tā zhù de zhège xiǎoqū, zài jiēbiān, zài gōngyuán, dōu néng kànjiàn hěnduō rén, tèbié shì lǎorén, zài duànliàn shēntǐ.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: In her residential community, on the streets, and in the parks, she can see many people, especially seniors, exercising.

segments.visual_notes: Animate icons for a community, a street, and a park, with small figures exercising in each.

segments.teaching_goal: Learn about common morning exercises in China and the '有的...有的...' structure.

narration.subtitle_en: What kind of exercises are they doing? This sentence tells us: Some of them do square dancing, and some practice tai chi. The structure [zh:有的...有的...] is used to list different activities done by different members of a group. It means 'some... others...'. Here, it contrasts two very popular forms of public exercise in China: [zh:广场舞] (square dancing) and [zh:太极拳] (tai chi).

on_screen_text.focus_text: 他们有的跳广场舞，有的打太极拳。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Tāmen yǒude tiào guǎngchǎngwǔ, yǒude dǎ tàijíquán.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Some of them do square dancing, and some practice tai chi.
on_screen_text.notes: 有的... 有的... (yǒude... yǒude...) means 'some... others...'.

segments.visual_notes: Show a split screen. On one side, an icon for square dancing. On the other, an icon for tai chi.

segments.teaching_goal: Learn the names of two popular Chinese public exercises.

narration.subtitle_en: Let's break down these two activities. First, [zh:广场舞]. [zh:广场] means 'public square' and [zh:舞] means 'dance'. So, it's literally 'square dance', a form of group dancing popular with middle-aged and older women. Second is [zh:太极拳]. This is a form of traditional Chinese martial art practiced for both its defense training and its health benefits. It's often called 'tai chi' in English.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 广场舞 / 太极拳
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: guǎngchǎngwǔ / tàijíquán
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: square dancing / tai chi

segments.visual_notes: Show a photo or stylized drawing of people doing square dancing and another of people practicing tai chi.

segments.teaching_goal: Understand the metaphorical use of '风景' (scenery).

narration.subtitle_en: The paragraph ends with a beautiful summary: These people who exercise in the morning have become a special 'scenery' of Beijing. The verb [zh:成为] means 'to become'. The word [zh:风景] literally means 'scenery' or 'landscape'. Here, it's used metaphorically to say that the sight of people exercising has become a unique and characteristic feature of the city, like a famous landmark. It's a cultural landscape.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 这些早晨锻炼的人们，成为北京特别的“风景”。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Zhèxiē zǎochen duànliàn de rénmen, chéngwéi Běijīng tèbié de “fēngjǐng”.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: These people who exercise in the morning have become a special 'scenery' of Beijing.
on_screen_text.notes: 风景 (fēngjǐng) is used metaphorically to mean a unique feature or sight of the city.

segments.visual_notes: Show a silhouette of Beijing's skyline with small figures doing tai chi in the foreground, inside a picture frame.

segments.teaching_goal: Understand the sentence that transitions into the dialogue.

narration.subtitle_en: This short sentence sets the scene for the conversation. It says: This morning, just as Lisa was about to go out, Li Wen came out of her room. The phrase [zh:正要] means 'to be just about to do something'. [zh:出门] is a common verb-object phrase meaning 'to go out' or 'to leave home'. It literally means 'to exit the door'. This line acts as a bridge from the background description to the start of the dialogue.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 这一天早上丽莎正要出门，李文从房间走出来。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Zhè yī tiān zǎoshang Lìshā zhèng yào chū mén, Lǐ Wén cóng fángjiān zǒu chūlái.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: This morning, just as Lisa was about to go out, Li Wen came out of her room.
on_screen_text.notes: 正要 (zhèngyào) means 'to be just about to...'

segments.visual_notes: Animate two doors. One opens with Lisa about to exit, and the other opens with Li Wen stepping out.

segments.teaching_goal: Understand how to express surprise about someone being up early.

narration.subtitle_en: Li Wen starts the conversation by expressing her surprise. She asks: Lisa, it's Saturday today. How come you're up so early? The word [zh:怎么] here is used to ask 'how' or 'why' in a surprised tone. [zh:这么早] means 'so early', and [zh:就] emphasizes that the action of getting up, [zh:起床], happened earlier than expected. It's a very natural way to express surprise about timing.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 丽莎，今天是星期六，怎么这么早就起床了？
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Lìshā, jīntiān shì xīngqīliù, zěnme zhème zǎo jiù qǐchuáng le?
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Lisa, it's Saturday today. How come you're up so early?

segments.visual_notes: Show a calendar icon on 'Saturday' and a clock showing an early time, with a question mark.

segments.teaching_goal: Learn to express an original plan versus an unexpected outcome with '本来...没想到...'.

narration.subtitle_en: Lisa explains her reason for being up early. She says: I was planning to learn tai chi with uncle and auntie, but I didn't expect them to have left so early. This sentence uses a great pattern: [zh:本来] introduces the original plan or intention. Then, [zh:没想到] introduces the unexpected reality, meaning 'didn't expect'. This structure is perfect for talking about plans that didn't go as anticipated.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 我本来想跟叔叔、阿姨去学太极拳，没想到他们那么早就出去了。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Wǒ běnlái xiǎng gēn shūshu, āyí qù xué tàijíquán, méi xiǎngdào tāmen nàme zǎo jiù chūqù le.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: I was planning to learn tai chi with uncle and auntie, but I didn't expect them to have left so early.
on_screen_text.notes: 本来 (běnlái)... 没想到 (méi xiǎngdào)... expresses an unexpected change of plans.

segments.visual_notes: Show a thought bubble with Lisa learning tai chi, then pop it and show the parents already gone.

segments.teaching_goal: Learn to use '以为' to express a mistaken assumption.

narration.subtitle_en: Li Wen expresses her surprise again, this time based on a past assumption. She says: I thought you only liked to do yoga. How come you're also interested in tai chi? The key word is [zh:以为], which means 'to think' or 'to assume', but it implies that the assumption was wrong. It's different from [zh:想] or [zh:觉得]. You use [zh:以为] when you realize what you thought was incorrect. The phrase [zh:对...有兴趣] means 'to be interested in...'

on_screen_text.focus_text: 我以为你只喜欢做瑜伽，怎么对太极拳也有兴趣？
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Wǒ yǐwéi nǐ zhǐ xǐhuān zuò yújiā, zěnme duì tàijíquán yě yǒu xìngqù?
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: I thought you only liked to do yoga. How come you're also interested in tai chi?
on_screen_text.notes: 以为 (yǐwéi) is used for a mistaken assumption.

segments.visual_notes: Show an icon of Lisa doing yoga with a checkmark, then an icon of tai chi with a question mark.

segments.teaching_goal: Learn to compare two things and list their benefits using '不但...而且...'.

narration.subtitle_en: Lisa explains her interest by comparing tai chi and yoga. She says: Tai chi is like yoga; not only is it good for your health, but the movements are also very beautiful. The structure [zh:不但...而且...] means 'not only... but also...'. It's used to present two related qualities or facts. Here, she lists two benefits of tai chi: it's good for the body, [zh:对身体有好处], and its movements, [zh:动作], are beautiful, [zh:很美].

on_screen_text.focus_text: 太极拳和瑜伽一样，不但对身体有好处，而且动作很美。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Tàijíquán hé yújiā yīyàng, bùdàn duì shēntǐ yǒu hǎochù, érqiě dòngzuò hěn měi.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Tai chi is like yoga; not only is it good for your health, but the movements are also very beautiful.
on_screen_text.notes: 不但 (bùdàn)... 而且 (érqiě)... means 'not only... but also...'

segments.visual_notes: Show two columns. Column 1: '对身体有好处' (Good for health). Column 2: '动作很美' (Beautiful movements). Put checkmarks in both.

segments.teaching_goal: Learn to point something out and make a suggestion.

narration.subtitle_en: Li Wen spots her parents and points them out to Lisa. She says: Lisa, look under the big tree. My dad and mom are practicing tai chi with those retired seniors! Go find them. The structure [zh:正在...呢] indicates an action in progress. They 'are practicing' tai chi right now. The sentence ends with [zh:你去找他们吧]. The particle [zh:吧] is used here to make a friendly suggestion.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 丽莎，你看大树下边，我爸、我妈正在跟那些退休老人打太极拳呢！你去找他们吧。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Lìshā, nǐ kàn dàshù xiàbian, wǒ bà, wǒ mā zhèngzài gēn nàxiē tuìxiū lǎorén dǎ tàijíquán ne! Nǐ qù zhǎo tāmen ba.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Lisa, look under the big tree. My dad and mom are practicing tai chi with those retired seniors! Go find them.
on_screen_text.notes: 正在...呢 (zhèngzài...ne) indicates an ongoing action. 吧 (ba) makes a suggestion.

segments.visual_notes: Animate a finger pointing towards a tree, where stick figures are doing tai chi.

segments.teaching_goal: Learn how to agree to a suggestion and give a compliment.

narration.subtitle_en: Lisa agrees and gives a compliment. She says: Okay, I'll go in a bit. They practice so well. [zh:好] is a simple agreement. [zh:一会儿就去] means 'I'll go in a moment'. Then she compliments their skill: [zh:他们打得真棒]. The particle [zh:得] connects the verb [zh:打] (to practice/play) with the description of how well they do it, [zh:真棒] (really great).

on_screen_text.focus_text: 好，一会儿就去。他们打得真棒。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Hǎo, yīhuìr jiù qù. Tāmen dǎ de zhēn bàng.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Okay, I'll go in a bit. They practice so well.

segments.visual_notes: Show a thumbs-up icon next to the people doing tai chi.

segments.teaching_goal: Understand Lisa's summary of her observations about exercise in China.

narration.subtitle_en: Lisa then shares her broader observation, connecting it back to the beginning of the text. She says: Every morning when I go for a walk, I see people exercising everywhere. Chinese people really value physical exercise! The word [zh:早晨] is another word for 'morning', similar to [zh:早上]. [zh:到处] means 'everywhere'. The final exclamation, [zh:中国人真重视锻炼身体啊], shows her strong impression that Chinese people truly 'value' or 'attach importance to' ([zh:重视]) physical exercise.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 我每天早晨出去散步，看见到处都是运动的人。中国人真重视锻炼身体啊！
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Wǒ měitiān zǎochen chūqù sànbù, kànjiàn dàochù dōu shì yùndòng de rén. Zhōngguórén zhēn zhòngshì duànliàn shēntǐ a!
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Every morning when I go for a walk, I see people exercising everywhere. Chinese people really value physical exercise!

segments.visual_notes: Show a map of a park with many small icons of people exercising scattered 'everywhere'.

segments.teaching_goal: Learn to express a growing trend with '越来越...' and ask about habits in other cultures.

narration.subtitle_en: Li Wen agrees and pivots the conversation. She says: Right, everyone is paying more and more attention to their health now. Hey, Lisa, how do Americans usually exercise? The phrase [zh:越来越] means 'more and more', indicating a trend. So, people are paying 'more and more attention' ([zh:越来越注意]) to health. Then, she uses [zh:哎] to casually get Lisa's attention before asking about American exercise habits, using the word [zh:一般] for 'usually' or 'generally'.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 对，现在大家都越来越注意身体健康了。哎，丽莎，美国人一般怎么锻炼身体？
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Duì, xiànzài dàjiā dōu yuèláiyuè zhùyì shēntǐ jiànkāng le. Āi, Lìshā, Měiguórén yībān zěnme duànliàn shēntǐ?
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Right, everyone is paying more and more attention to their health now. Hey, Lisa, how do Americans usually exercise?

segments.visual_notes: Show an upward trending arrow labeled '越来越注意健康'. Then show a US flag next to a question mark.

segments.teaching_goal: To understand how to list common types of exercise and describe one's own exercise habits.

narration.subtitle_en: This sentence lists common forms of exercise. It starts with [zh:一般是], meaning 'usually it is'. The activities are [zh:跑步] (running), [zh:游泳] (swimming), and [zh:打球] (playing ball). The word [zh:等等] means 'and so on'. The speaker then adds that some people go to the [zh:健身房], or 'gym'. Finally, she shares her own routine: [zh:我平常做瑜伽], 'I usually do yoga', and [zh:有的时候也跑步], 'sometimes I also run'.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 一般是跑步、游泳、打球等等，有些人也去健身房。我平常做瑜伽，有的时候也跑步。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Yìbān shì pǎobù, yóuyǒng, dǎqiú děngděng, yǒuxiē rén yě qù jiànshēnfáng. Wǒ píngcháng zuò yújiā, yǒude shíhou yě pǎobù.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Usually it's running, swimming, playing ball, and so on. Some people also go to the gym. I usually do yoga, and sometimes I run too.

highlight_words.explanation_en: The verb-object phrase for 'to run' or 'running'.
The verb-object phrase for 'to swim' or 'swimming'.
Literally 'health-body-room', a place for working out.
A phonetic borrowing for the word 'yoga'.

segments.visual_notes: Animate icons for running, swimming, playing ball, and a gym appearing next to the words.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn how to use '难怪' (nánguài) to mean 'no wonder'.

narration.subtitle_en: Here we learn a very useful phrase, [zh:难怪], which means 'no wonder'. You use it when you suddenly understand the reason for something. The rest of the sentence is [zh:你的身体这么健康]. [zh:你的身体] means 'your body' or 'your health', and [zh:这么健康] means 'so healthy'. Altogether, it means 'No wonder you are so healthy!', connecting the cause (exercise) to the effect (good health).

on_screen_text.focus_text: 难怪你的身体这么健康。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Nánguài nǐ de shēntǐ zhème jiànkāng.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: No wonder you are so healthy.

highlight_words.explanation_en: Used to express that something is not surprising after learning the reason.
Refers to the physical body and can also imply one's state of health.
An adjective meaning 'healthy' or 'in good health'.

segments.visual_notes: Highlight '难怪' and show a lightbulb icon turning on to represent realization.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn how to give advice using the '除了...以外，还...' (besides..., also...) structure.

narration.subtitle_en: This sentence gives advice on health. It starts with [zh:我觉得], 'I think'. The core structure is [zh:除了...以外，还...], meaning 'besides..., also...'. First, [zh:如果希望身体健康] means 'if you want to be healthy'. Then comes the structure: [zh:除了多运动以外], 'besides exercising more', [zh:还应该注意饮食], 'you should also pay attention to your diet'. Key words are [zh:应该] for 'should', [zh:注意] for 'pay attention to', and [zh:饮食] for 'diet'.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 我觉得如果希望身体健康，除了多运动以外，还应该注意饮食。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Wǒ juéde rúguǒ xīwàng shēntǐ jiànkāng, chúle duō yùndòng yǐwài, hái yīnggāi zhùyì yǐnshí.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: I think if you want to be healthy, besides exercising more, you should also pay attention to your diet.

highlight_words.explanation_en: A conjunction used to introduce a conditional clause.
A structure used to introduce an exception or an addition.
A modal verb indicating obligation or suggestion.
Literally 'drink-eat', referring to one's food and drink, or diet.

segments.visual_notes: Visually connect '除了' and '还' to show the 'besides... also...' relationship.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn the phrase '一点儿都不' (yìdiǎnr dōu bù) for 'not at all'.

narration.subtitle_en: This is a question expressing surprise. The key phrase is [zh:一点儿都不], which means 'not at all'. So, [zh:你一点儿都不胖] means 'You're not fat at all'. The word [zh:胖] means 'fat'. The second part of the sentence, [zh:还需要注意饮食吗], asks, 'Do you still need to pay attention to your diet?'. [zh:还] means 'still' and [zh:需要] means 'to need'.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 你一点儿都不胖，还需要注意饮食吗？
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Nǐ yìdiǎnr dōu bú pàng, hái xūyào zhùyì yǐnshí ma?
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: You're not fat at all. Do you still need to pay attention to your diet?

highlight_words.explanation_en: An emphatic structure used to negate something completely.
An adjective to describe someone as being overweight.
A verb indicating necessity.

segments.visual_notes: Emphasize '一点儿都不' with a visual that crosses out the word '胖'.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn how to clarify a point and express an opinion about health.

narration.subtitle_en: This line clarifies a misconception. It starts with [zh:注意饮食不等于减肥], 'Paying attention to diet isn't the same as losing weight'. [zh:等于] means 'to equal', and [zh:减肥] is 'to lose weight'. Then, an opinion is given using the structure [zh:只要...就...], 'as long as...'. [zh:只要身体健康就好] means 'as long as you're healthy, that's good enough'. The sentence concludes with [zh:胖瘦不重要], 'being fat or thin isn't important'. [zh:胖瘦] is a great word combining 'fat' and 'thin' to mean 'weight'.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 注意饮食不等于减肥。我认为只要身体健康就好，胖瘦不重要。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Zhùyì yǐnshí bù děngyú jiǎnféi. Wǒ rènwéi zhǐyào shēntǐ jiànkāng jiù hǎo, pàng shòu bú zhòngyào.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Paying attention to diet isn't the same as losing weight. I think as long as you're healthy, being fat or thin isn't important.

highlight_words.explanation_en: Used to state that two things are equivalent.
Literally 'reduce fat', the common term for dieting or losing weight.
A structure where the first part introduces a necessary condition for the result in the second part.
A compound word from 'fat' and 'thin' that refers to a person's weight or build.

segments.visual_notes: Show a scale with '健康' (health) balancing on it, while '胖' (fat) and '瘦' (thin) are shown as less important.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn how to describe being too busy to eat well and ask for advice.

narration.subtitle_en: This line describes a common problem. [zh:我工作忙，没时间好好儿吃饭] means 'I'm busy with work and don't have time to eat properly'. [zh:好好儿] means 'properly' or 'well'. The next phrase, [zh:常常随便乱吃], vividly describes poor eating habits. [zh:常常] is 'often', and [zh:随便乱吃] means to eat casually and randomly, without a proper meal. Finally, the speaker asks for advice: [zh:你是怎么注意饮食的呢], 'How do you pay attention to your diet?'

on_screen_text.focus_text: 我工作忙，没时间好好儿吃饭，常常随便乱吃。你是怎么注意饮食的呢？
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Wǒ gōngzuò máng, méi shíjiān hǎohāor chīfàn, chángcháng suíbiàn luàn chī. Nǐ shì zěnme zhùyì yǐnshí de ne?
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: I'm busy with work and don't have time to eat properly, so I often eat whatever. How do you pay attention to your diet?

highlight_words.explanation_en: An adverb indicating that an action should be done properly or to a good standard.
An adverb of frequency, meaning 'frequently' or 'often'.
A colloquial phrase describing eating without care for what, when, or how.

segments.visual_notes: Show a clock spinning fast next to a person grabbing random junk food to illustrate being busy and eating poorly.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn basic dietary advice: drink more water and eat more vegetables and fruits.

narration.subtitle_en: Here begins a series of tips for a healthy diet. This first part is very simple and direct. It uses the structure [zh:多] plus a verb, which means 'do more of something'. So, [zh:多喝水] means 'drink more water'. And [zh:多吃青菜、水果] means 'eat more green vegetables and fruits'. [zh:青菜] refers to green, leafy vegetables, and [zh:水果] is the general word for fruit.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 多喝水，多吃青菜、水果。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Duō hē shuǐ, duō chī qīngcài, shuǐguǒ.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Drink more water, eat more vegetables and fruits.

highlight_words.explanation_en: Used before a verb, it means to do that action more.
A common term for green, leafy vegetables.
Literally 'water fruit', the general term for all fruits.

segments.visual_notes: Show an animated glass of water filling up and a basket of colorful fruits and vegetables.

segments.segment_title: Dietary Advice Part 2: The Importance of Breakfast
segments.teaching_goal: To understand the importance of breakfast using '即使...也...' and '而且'.

narration.subtitle_en: The advice continues with [zh:另外], meaning 'in addition'. Here we see the structure [zh:即使...也...], which means 'even if...'. The sentence [zh:即使非常忙，也一定要吃早饭] means 'even if you're very busy, you must eat breakfast'. [zh:一定] emphasizes that it's a must. Then, [zh:而且] adds another point: 'furthermore, breakfast should be nutritious'. The word for nutritious is [zh:营养].

on_screen_text.focus_text: 另外，即使非常忙，也一定要吃早饭，而且早饭要有营养。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Lìngwài, jíshǐ fēicháng máng, yě yídìng yào chī zǎofàn, érqiě zǎofàn yào yǒu yíngyǎng.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Also, even if you're very busy, you must eat breakfast, and it should be nutritious.

highlight_words.explanation_en: A conjunction to add another point.
A structure that presents a hypothetical scenario and the unchanging result.
An adverb used to add emphasis and certainty.
Refers to nourishment or being nutritious.

segments.visual_notes: Highlight '即使' and '也' to show the connection. Show a nutritious breakfast (e.g., egg, milk, bread).

segments.segment_title: Dietary Advice Part 3: A Full Lunch
segments.teaching_goal: To learn how to talk about lunch and the reason for eating a full meal.

narration.subtitle_en: Next up is lunch, or [zh:午饭]. The advice is [zh:要吃饱], which means 'you should eat until you are full'. [zh:吃饱] is a very common phrase. The sentence then gives a reason using [zh:因为], 'because'. The reason is: [zh:下午还要学习和工作], 'in the afternoon, you still have to study and work'. This explains why a full lunch is necessary for energy.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 午饭要吃饱，因为下午还要学习和工作；
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Wǔfàn yào chībǎo, yīnwèi xiàwǔ hái yào xuéxí hé gōngzuò;
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: You should eat a full lunch because you still have to study and work in the afternoon.

highlight_words.explanation_en: The meal eaten in the middle of the day.
A resultative complement where '饱' (bǎo) means 'full' or 'satiated'.
A conjunction used to introduce a reason or cause.

segments.visual_notes: Show a full plate of food for lunch, followed by icons for studying (book) and working (laptop).

segments.segment_title: Dietary Advice Part 4: A Small Dinner
segments.teaching_goal: To learn how to advise eating less for dinner and explain the consequences using '不然' and '越来越'.

narration.subtitle_en: For dinner, [zh:晚饭], the advice is to [zh:少吃一点], 'eat a little less'. Then comes a warning with the word [zh:不然], which means 'otherwise'. What happens otherwise? [zh:会越来越胖], 'you will get fatter and fatter'. The structure [zh:越来越] is very useful for describing a trend, meaning 'more and more'.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 晚饭就要少吃一点，不然会越来越胖，
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Wǎnfàn jiù yào shǎo chī yìdiǎn, bùrán huì yuèláiyuè pàng,
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: For dinner, you should eat a little less, otherwise you'll get fatter and fatter,

highlight_words.explanation_en: The evening meal.
A conjunction used to introduce a negative consequence.
A structure used before an adjective or verb to indicate a progressive change.

segments.visual_notes: Show a small portion of food for dinner. Then, an arrow pointing from '不然' to a silhouette that gets progressively wider.

segments.segment_title: Dietary Advice Part 5: The Reason for a Small Dinner
segments.teaching_goal: To learn how to explain a reason using '因为' and the structure '离...太近了'.

narration.subtitle_en: This final part explains why you should eat less for dinner. It starts with [zh:因为], 'because'. The key structure is [zh:离...太近了], meaning 'it's too close to...'. The preposition [zh:离] indicates distance in time or space. Here, it's too close to [zh:上床睡觉的时间], 'the time to go to bed'. [zh:上床睡觉] literally means 'get into bed and sleep'. So, the full reason is that dinnertime is too close to bedtime.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 因为离上床睡觉的时间太近了。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: yīnwèi lí shàngchuáng shuìjiào de shíjiān tài jìn le.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: because it's too close to bedtime.

highlight_words.explanation_en: A preposition used to talk about the distance between two places, times, or points.
A common phrase for going to bed to sleep.
The '太...了' structure expresses 'too' of something. Here, 'too close'.

segments.visual_notes: Show a clock with the hands for dinner and bedtime very close together.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn a traditional Chinese saying about how to eat three meals a day.

narration.subtitle_en: This line introduces a well-known Chinese saying. It begins with [zh:我们中国人有一句话], 'We Chinese people have a saying'. The saying itself is a catchy, parallel phrase: [zh:早餐要吃好], 'Eat a good breakfast'; [zh:午餐要吃饱], 'Eat a full lunch'; and [zh:晚餐要吃少], 'Eat a small dinner'. Notice the use of [zh:餐] which is slightly more formal than [zh:饭], fitting for a traditional saying.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 我们中国人有一句话：早餐要吃好，午餐要吃饱，晚餐要吃少。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Wǒmen Zhōngguórén yǒu yí jù huà: zǎocān yào chī hǎo, wǔcān yào chī bǎo, wǎncān yào chī shǎo.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: We Chinese have a saying: 'Eat a good breakfast, a full lunch, and a small dinner.'

highlight_words.explanation_en: Literally 'one sentence of speech', used to introduce a saying or a phrase.
A more formal way to refer to the three daily meals compared to 早饭/午饭/晚饭.

segments.visual_notes: Display the saying in elegant calligraphy-style font, with icons for a quality breakfast, a full lunch, and a small dinner below each part.

segments.teaching_goal: To understand the difference between using '餐' and '饭' for meals.

narration.subtitle_en: Let's look at the words for meals. You've heard both [zh:早饭] and [zh:早餐] for 'breakfast'. Similarly, there's [zh:午饭] and [zh:午餐] for 'lunch', and [zh:晚饭] and [zh:晚餐] for 'dinner'. The main difference is formality. The character [zh:餐] is generally more formal than [zh:饭]. You'll often see [zh:餐] in writing, on menus, or in set phrases and sayings. In everyday spoken Chinese, [zh:饭] is extremely common. For the most part, you can use them interchangeably.

on_screen_text.focus_text:
早餐 / 午餐 / 晚餐
早饭 / 午饭 / 晚饭
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin:
zǎocān / wǔcān / wǎncān
zǎofàn / wǔfàn / wǎnfàn
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en:
Breakfast / Lunch / Dinner (Formal)
Breakfast / Lunch / Dinner (Colloquial)
on_screen_text.notes: 餐 (cān) is more formal than 饭 (fàn).

highlight_words.explanation_en: A character for 'meal' that is often used in more formal or written contexts.
A character for 'meal' or 'cooked rice' that is very common in daily conversation.

segments.visual_notes: Show two columns, one labeled 'Formal (餐)' and the other 'Everyday (饭)', with the corresponding meal words listed below.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn how to say something is 'pretty much the same' using '差不多一样'.

narration.subtitle_en: This phrase connects the Chinese saying to the previous advice. The basic structure is [zh:跟...一样], which means 'the same as...'. Here, it's [zh:跟你说的], 'the same as what you said'. The word [zh:差不多] is added in the middle to mean 'almost' or 'pretty much'. So, the entire phrase [zh:跟你说的差不多一样] means 'It's pretty much the same as what you said.' It's a great way to express strong agreement.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 跟你说的差不多一样。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: gēn nǐ shuō de chàbuduō yíyàng.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: pretty much the same as what you said.

highlight_words.explanation_en: A comparative structure to say two things are the same.
An adverb that indicates two things are very close but not exactly identical.

segments.visual_notes: Show two speech bubbles with similar content, with an '≈' (almost equal to) sign between them.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn how to agree with a statement and speculate on the reason.

narration.subtitle_en: This line shows agreement and offers a logical conclusion. It starts with [zh:真的？], 'Really?'. Then, [zh:大家都这么说] means 'Everyone says so'. [zh:大家] is 'everyone'. The second part speculates on the reason: [zh:可能是有科学道理的]. [zh:可能] means 'maybe' or 'possibly'. [zh:科学道理] means 'scientific reason'. The [zh:是...的] structure is used here to emphasize the existence of this scientific reason.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 真的？大家都这么说，可能是有科学道理的。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Zhēn de? Dàjiā dōu zhème shuō, kěnéng shì yǒu kēxué dàolǐ de.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Really? Everyone says that, maybe there's a scientific reason for it.

highlight_words.explanation_en: A pronoun used to refer to everybody.
An adverb expressing possibility.
The noun for 'science'.
Refers to a reason, logic, or principle behind something.

segments.visual_notes: Show a group of people nodding in agreement, then a brain icon with a gear and a DNA strand to represent 'scientific reason'.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn how to broaden a topic and use the word '必须' (bìxū) for 'must'.

narration.subtitle_en: This is a concluding thought that widens the discussion. It starts with [zh:我觉得], 'I think'. Then, [zh:想要身体好] means 'if you want to be healthy'. The next part, [zh:在其他方面也必须注意], means 'you must also pay attention in other aspects'. [zh:其他方面] means 'other aspects'. The key word here is [zh:必须], which means 'must' or 'have to'. It's a strong word that indicates necessity.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 我觉得想要身体好，在其他方面也必须注意。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Wǒ juéde xiǎngyào shēntǐ hǎo, zài qítā fāngmiàn yě bìxū zhùyì.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: I think if you want to be healthy, you must also pay attention to other aspects.

highlight_words.explanation_en: Expresses a desire or wish to do something.
'其他' means 'other', and '方面' means 'aspect' or 'side'.
A modal verb expressing a strong, unavoidable obligation.

segments.visual_notes: Show a central icon for 'health', with lines branching out to 'exercise', 'diet', and then a '...' icon labeled '其他方面' (other aspects).

segments.segment_title: Vocabulary: 必须 vs. 不必
segments.teaching_goal: To learn the word '必须' (must) and its correct negative forms, '不必' and '不用'.

narration.subtitle_en: Let's take a closer look at [zh:必须], which means 'must'. It expresses a strong obligation, like in the sentence [zh:你必须吃早饭], 'You must eat breakfast'. Now, how do you say 'must not' or 'don't have to'? Be careful, you cannot say '[zh:不必须]'. The correct negative forms are [zh:不必] or [zh:不用]. Both mean 'need not' or 'don't have to'. For example, 'You don't need to pay rent' is [zh:你不必付房租]. So remember, the opposite of the obligation [zh:必须] is the lack of necessity, [zh:不必].

on_screen_text.focus_text:
必须 (must)
不必 / 不用 (need not)
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin:
bìxū
búbì / búyòng
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en:
Must
Need not / Don't have to
on_screen_text.notes: The negative of 必须 (bìxū) is 不必 (búbì) or 不用 (búyòng), not '不必须'.

highlight_words.explanation_en: Expresses strong obligation.
The negative form of 必须, expressing lack of necessity.
A common alternative to 不必, also meaning 'don't have to'.

segments.visual_notes: Show '必须' with a green checkmark and '不必须' with a red X. Then show arrows from the red X to '不必' and '不用', both with green checkmarks.

segments.teaching_goal: To teach how to confirm understanding by rephrasing and using the tag question '对不对？'.

narration.subtitle_en: The speaker confirms their understanding by rephrasing the previous point. '你的意思' means 'you mean' or 'your meaning is'. '是要有良好的生活习惯' means 'is to have good lifestyle habits'. The phrase ends with [zh:对不对], a common tag question that means 'right?' or 'is that correct?'. It's formed by stating the positive and negative form of the adjective '对'.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 你的意思是要有良好的生活习惯，对不对？
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Nǐ de yìsi shì yào yǒu liánghǎo de shēnghuó xíguàn, duì bu duì?
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: You mean to have good lifestyle habits, right?

highlight_words.explanation_en: Refers to the meaning or intention behind someone's words.
A compound word: 生活 (shēnghuó) means 'life' and 习惯 (xíguàn) means 'habit'.
A tag question used to seek confirmation, similar to 'correct?' or 'isn't it?'.

segments.visual_notes: Animate the text '对不对？' to pop up at the end of the sentence to emphasize its function as a question.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn how to list positive and negative habits related to a healthy lifestyle.

narration.subtitle_en: The response starts with [zh:对], meaning 'Right.' Then, it lists several habits. Notice the pattern '不 + Verb' for things one shouldn't do: [zh:不吸烟] 'don't smoke,' and [zh:不喝酒] 'don't drink.' This is followed by good habits: [zh:早睡早起] 'sleep early and wake up early.' Finally, a suggestion: [zh:最好不要熬夜] 'it's best not to stay up late.' '最好' means 'it's best'.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 对。不吸烟，不喝酒，早睡早起，最好不要熬夜。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Duì. Bù xīyān, bù hējiǔ, zǎo shuì zǎo qǐ, zuìhǎo búyào áoyè.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Right. Don't smoke, don't drink, sleep early and wake up early, and it's best not to stay up late.

highlight_words.explanation_en: A verb-object phrase: 吸 (xī) means 'to inhale' and 烟 (yān) means 'smoke' or 'cigarette'.
A verb-object phrase: 喝 (hē) means 'to drink' and 酒 (jiǔ) means 'alcohol'.

highlight_words.translation: sleep early and wake up early
highlight_words.explanation_en: A common four-character phrase describing a healthy routine.
To stay up all night or very late, often for work or study.

segments.visual_notes: Use icons for each habit (no smoking, no drinking, sleeping, staying up late) next to the corresponding Chinese phrase.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn how to state that everything is fine except for one specific thing, using the '就是...做不到' structure.

narration.subtitle_en: Here, the speaker explains their personal challenge. [zh:别的都没问题] means 'I have no problem with the others.' '别的' means 'other things'. The key structure is [zh:就是...做不到], which means 'it's just that... I can't do it.' It's used to single out the one exception. [zh:做不到] is a potential complement, meaning 'cannot manage to do' or 'unable to achieve'.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 别的都没问题，就是不熬夜我做不到。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Bié de dōu méi wèntí, jiùshì bù áoyè wǒ zuò bu dào.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: I have no problem with the others, but I just can't manage not staying up late.

highlight_words.explanation_en: A pronoun used to refer to other, unspecified things or people.
Used here for emphasis to introduce an exception or a specific point.
A potential complement indicating inability to complete an action.

segments.visual_notes: Highlight '别的' and '就是不熬夜' in different colors to show the contrast.

segments.teaching_goal: To understand and use the common idiom '开夜车' for staying up late to work or study.

narration.subtitle_en: Let's look at the idiom [zh:开夜车]. Literally, it means 'to drive a night car.' However, its figurative meaning is 'to burn the midnight oil' or 'to stay up late working or studying.' This is a very common and vivid expression in Chinese. For example, if you have an exam tomorrow, you might tell your friend, '我今晚得开夜车了' (I have to pull an all-nighter tonight).

on_screen_text.focus_text: 开夜车
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: kāi yèchē
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: to burn the midnight oil; to stay up late working or studying
on_screen_text.notes: Literal: to drive a night car

highlight_words.explanation_en: An idiom that literally means 'to drive a night car' but figuratively means to work or study late into the night.
highlight_words.character_insight_en: 开 (kāi) means 'to drive', 夜 (yè) means 'night', and 车 (chē) means 'car'. The image is of working through the night like a night-shift driver.

segments.visual_notes: Show an animation of a car driving at night, which then transforms into a person studying at a desk with a clock showing a late hour.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn how to explain the reason for staying up late, using '得' and '准备'.

narration.subtitle_en: Now the speaker gives the reason for staying up. '我晚上常常' means 'I often at night'. The modal verb [zh:得] means 'must' or 'have to'. Then we see our new idiom, [zh:开夜车], 'to burn the midnight oil'. Why? [zh:准备考研究生] 'to prepare for the graduate entrance exams.' '准备' means 'to prepare,' and '研究生' means 'graduate student'.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 我晚上常常得开夜车准备考研究生。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Wǒ wǎnshang chángcháng děi kāi yèchē zhǔnbèi kǎo yánjiūshēng.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: I often have to burn the midnight oil to prepare for the graduate entrance exams.

highlight_words.explanation_en: A modal verb indicating necessity or obligation.
To get ready for something.
A student pursuing a master's or doctoral degree.

segments.visual_notes: Animate a stack of books growing taller next to the phrase '准备考研究生'.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn how to give strong advice using '必须' and '尽可能'.

narration.subtitle_en: This sentence gives a piece of advice. It starts with '那你', which means 'In that case, you...'. The core of the advice uses [zh:必须], a strong word for 'must'. Its negative forms are '不必' or '不用', meaning 'need not'. The adverb [zh:尽可能] means 'as much as possible'. The action is '找时间补充睡眠', which means 'find time to supplement sleep' or 'catch up on sleep'.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 那你必须尽可能找时间补充睡眠。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Nà nǐ bìxū jǐn kěnéng zhǎo shíjiān bǔchōng shuìmián.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Then you must find time to catch up on sleep as much as possible.

highlight_words.explanation_en: A strong modal verb indicating obligation. Its negative form is 不必 (búbì) or 不用 (búyòng).
An adverb used to express doing something to the greatest extent possible.
Literally 'to supplement sleep'. 补充 (bǔchōng) means 'to supplement' and 睡眠 (shuìmián) is the formal word for 'sleep'.

segments.visual_notes: Highlight '必须' in a bold color to emphasize the strength of the advice. Show a small clock icon next to '找时间'.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn how to accept advice and state a potential negative consequence using '要不然'.

narration.subtitle_en: The speaker agrees with the advice. [zh:你说得对] means 'You're right.' Then, '我是得注意了' emphasizes the need to pay attention; the '是' adds emphasis. The second part introduces a consequence with [zh:要不然], which means 'otherwise'. The consequence is '我的两只眼睛都快变成熊猫眼了' - 'my two eyes will soon turn into panda eyes.' The structure '快...了' indicates something is about to happen.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 你说得对，我是得注意了，要不然我的两只眼睛都快变成熊猫眼了。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Nǐ shuō de duì, wǒ shì děi zhùyì le, yàoburán wǒ de liǎng zhī yǎnjing dōu kuài biànchéng xióngmāoyǎn le.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: You're right, I really need to pay attention to it, otherwise my two eyes will soon turn into panda eyes.

highlight_words.explanation_en: A verb meaning to take note of or be careful about something.
A conjunction used to introduce a negative consequence if a condition is not met.
A verb indicating a change or transformation into something else.

segments.visual_notes: Split the screen. On one side, show a checkmark for '你说得对'. On the other, show an arrow pointing from '要不然' to an image of '熊猫眼'.

segments.teaching_goal: To understand the cultural phrase '熊猫眼' to describe dark circles under the eyes.

narration.subtitle_en: Let's explore the fun phrase [zh:熊猫眼]. It literally means 'panda eyes.' In Chinese, this is a very common and descriptive way to talk about the dark circles you get under your eyes from lack of sleep. The word for panda is [zh:熊猫]. It's a great example of how culture and imagery are embedded in the language. So if your friend looks tired, you can jokingly ask if they have 'panda eyes'.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 熊猫眼
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: xióngmāoyǎn
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: panda eyes (dark circles under the eyes)
on_screen_text.notes: A vivid metaphor for looking tired.

highlight_words.explanation_en: A colloquial term for dark circles under one's eyes, caused by lack of sleep.
highlight_words.character_insight_en: 熊猫 (xióngmāo) is 'panda' and 眼 (yǎn) is 'eye'. The phrase directly compares the dark circles to a panda's distinct eye patches.

segments.visual_notes: Show a picture of a panda next to a cartoon drawing of a person with exaggerated dark circles under their eyes. Draw a connecting line between the panda's eyes and the person's.

segments.teaching_goal: To understand that some two-syllable words can be shortened to one syllable in formal Chinese.

narration.subtitle_en: Let's dive into our first grammar point. In formal or written Chinese, you'll often see two-syllable words shortened to just one syllable. This makes the language more concise. For example, the word for 'already', [zh:已经], can be shortened to just [zh:已]. In the text, we saw the phrase [zh:已退休], which means 'already retired.' Here, [zh:已] is used instead of [zh:已经]. Let's look at a few more common examples of this.

rows.example_pinyin: Fángdōng fūqī èr rén yǐ tuìxiū.
rows.example_english: The landlord couple has already retired.
You should pay attention to your diet.
Because of time issues, the meeting is canceled.

rows.example_pinyin: Tā hěn cōngming, dàn bú yònggōng.
rows.example_english: He is smart, but not diligent.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn the formal word '与' (yǔ), which means 'and' or 'with'.

narration.subtitle_en: Next, let's look at the word [zh:与]. This is a more formal way to say 'and' or 'with', similar to [zh:和] and [zh:跟]. You'll encounter it more often in writing or formal speech. In our text, the sentence [zh:女儿李文在博物馆工作，与父母住在一起] means 'Their daughter, Li Wen, works at a museum and lives *with* her parents.' Here, [zh:与] connects 'parents' to the action of living together. It can also be used to link two nouns, just like 'and' in English. For example, [zh:教师与学生] means 'teachers and students'.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 李文与父母住在一起。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Lǐ Wén yǔ fùmǔ zhù zài yìqǐ.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Li Wen lives with her parents.
on_screen_text.notes: 与 is a formal equivalent of 和 (hé) and 跟 (gēn).

segments.visual_notes: Show '与' on screen, with '和' and '跟' appearing next to it, labeled as 'more common/spoken'.

segments.teaching_goal: To master the '只要...就...' structure to express a necessary condition and its result.

narration.subtitle_en: Now for a very useful structure: [zh:只要...就...]. This pattern means 'as long as... then...'. The part after [zh:只要] introduces the one necessary condition, and the part after [zh:就] states the result that will happen if that condition is met. Lisa uses this in the dialogue, saying [zh:只要身体健康就好], which means 'As long as you're healthy, that's good enough.' This implies that health is the most important condition. Let's see another example: [zh:只要你好好儿学习，你的中文就一定会进步]. 'As long as you study hard, your Chinese will definitely improve.'

on_screen_text.focus_text: 只要 + [Necessary Condition], 就 + [Result]
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: zhǐyào..., jiù...
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: As long as..., then...

grammar_points.pattern: 只要身体健康就好，胖瘦不重要。
grammar_points.pattern_pinyin: zhǐyào shēntǐ jiànkāng jiù hǎo, pàngshòu bù zhòngyào.
grammar_points.explanation_en: From the text: 'As long as you're healthy, that's good; being fat or thin isn't important.'

grammar_points.pattern: 只要你好好儿用功学习,你的中文就一定会进步。
grammar_points.pattern_pinyin: Zhǐyào nǐ hǎohāor yònggōng xuéxí, nǐ de Zhōngwén jiù yídìng huì jìnbù.
grammar_points.explanation_en: Example: 'As long as you study diligently, your Chinese will definitely improve.'

segments.visual_notes: Animate the structure. A key icon appears next to '只要', and an arrow points from the condition to the result next to '就'.

segments.teaching_goal: To use the '即使...也...' structure to talk about hypothetical or extreme situations.

narration.subtitle_en: Our next pattern, [zh:即使...也...], is used to say 'even if...'. [zh:即使] introduces a hypothetical or even extreme situation, and [zh:也] introduces the result or conclusion that remains true despite that situation. In the dialogue, Lisa gives advice on diet: [zh:即使非常忙，也一定要吃早饭]. This means 'Even if you are extremely busy, you must eat breakfast.' The busyness doesn't change the need to eat breakfast. Another example could be: [zh:即使下大雨，我也要去上课], 'Even if it's raining heavily, I still have to go to class.'

on_screen_text.focus_text: 即使 + [Hypothetical Scenario], (Subject) + 也 + [Result]
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: jíshǐ..., yě...
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Even if...

grammar_points.pattern: 即使非常忙，也一定要吃早饭。
grammar_points.pattern_pinyin: jíshǐ fēicháng máng, yě yídìng yào chī zǎofàn.
grammar_points.explanation_en: From the text: 'Even if you're very busy, you must eat breakfast.'

grammar_points.pattern: 即使熬夜不睡觉,我也要把这篇文章写完。
grammar_points.pattern_pinyin: Jíshǐ áoyè bù shuìjiào, wǒ yě yào bǎ zhè piān wénzhāng xiě wán.
grammar_points.explanation_en: Example: 'Even if I have to stay up all night, I will finish writing this article.'

segments.visual_notes: Show an icon representing an extreme condition (like a storm or a very busy person) for the '即使' clause, and an icon showing a determined action for the '也' clause.

segments.teaching_goal: To review the key grammar patterns and vocabulary from the lesson.

narration.subtitle_en: Great job! Let's quickly review what we've learned about lifestyle and health. We discussed how to stay healthy with exercise and diet, and we covered some important grammar. Remember the key patterns and vocabulary as you practice.
